cream-colored knitted sweater.description: A close-up of a cream-colored knitted sweater with ribbed detailing around the neckline and sleeves, showcasing a textured, cozy design.

café interior.description: A dimly lit café with silhouettes of people seated at tables, a chandelier hanging above, and arched windows showing the street outside, emphasizing the contrast between the dark interior and the brighter exterior.

woman in black top and white scarf.description: A woman with gray hair pulled back, wearing a black top and a white scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, airy room with white columns and soft natural lighting, providing commentary on brand desire and Zara's unique approach to marketing and production.

woman in gray beanie and green coat.description: A woman with long dark hair, wearing a gray knitted beanie and a green coat, walking toward the camera on a city sidewalk in front of the ZARA store, stating in French (translated to English) that she visits Zara 'three to four times a week'.

woman in camouflage jacket and sunglasse.description: A woman with long dark hair, wearing a camouflage jacket and large round sunglasses, standing on a city sidewalk, touching her face as she speaks to the camera, stating in English that she shops at Zara 'about three times a week'.

woman with curly blonde hair.description: A woman with curly blonde hair, wearing a dark denim jacket over a purple top, standing on a city sidewalk, gesturing with her hands as she speaks directly to the camera, mentioning 'sometimes in the United States...'.

raw_segments: The video opens with a street-level view of a ZARA storefront, its large glass windows displaying mannequins in dark, stylish outfits, including a floral dress and a black jacket with floral embroidery. As the camera pans across the display, capturing reflections of the street and buildings, a female narrator explains that Zara understood how showcases give the brand its image, integrating this strategy from the start, and that these showcases are designed in great secret in the bunker of A Coruña. The scene then shifts to a modern glass building with a green lawn in front, possibly the Inditex premises mentioned by the narrator. This transitions to an indoor setting where Mark Tungate, a man with gray hair and glasses, wearing a black turtleneck, is seated at a desk with a laptop and his book titled 'FASHION BRANDS'. He types, then turns to speak directly to the camera with expressive hand gestures, describing his experience entering Inditex. With a French accent, he likens it to arriving at a villain's lair in a "James Bond movie," detailing a lift, a lobby where "everything was white, white, white, very minimalist," and "a whole street of showcases which are called pilot stores," comparing the atmosphere to a "ghost town." The background features a bookshelf, creating a scholarly atmosphere. The video then cuts to a 3D-rendered fashion showroom, visually echoing Tungate's description, with white mannequins in black outfits standing in a minimalist space with pink abstract light patterns on the walls, suggesting a digital or conceptual design environment.

The video opens with a sleek, minimalist 3D-rendered fashion showroom, showcasing white mannequins in black and white outfits against a clean white space with pink abstract light patterns, suggesting a digital design environment. As the scene transitions to a dimly lit workshop, a woman in a colorful tropical-print shirt sketches while a man in a beige sweater and scarf examines printed garments featuring surfers and birds, gesturing toward them in a collaborative studio setting. A male voice-over, possibly with a French accent, explains that "they create with their team the image that will be the image of the season and that will be transmitted to all the shops around the world." The narrative then shifts to real-world ZARA storefronts on bustling city streets, with pedestrians and a yellow taxi passing by, followed by a close-up of a mannequin inside. A female voice-over, with an American accent, describes these as "ghost stores" that are "unique in the fashion world," emphasizing that "the brand cares about getting the best locations," reportedly paying "300 million euros for the store located on Fifth Avenue in New York." She continues, stating that "Zara invests heavily in its stores" and that "those displays you immediately notice are located in the nicest neighborhoods," serving as "Zara's best publicity." Exterior shots of ZARA locations, including one on Rue de Rivoli, are shown, leading to a wide view of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris with traffic flowing through the Champs-Élysées. The final scenes show a blurred, warm-lit café with outdoor seating, where a woman in a black coat enters, creating a cozy urban evening atmosphere, as the female narrator explains, "To understand the Zara system, we take an appointment in Paris with a man working for a competing company," who has "studied Zara's operations" for months and finds "the brand's inner workings are frightening." Throughout the segment, a subtle, investigative background music track plays.

The segment opens with a dimly lit, cozy café featuring outdoor seating with white tablecloths, greenery, candles, and a 'Paparazzi' sign visible through the glass. A female voice begins, stating, "He agreed to meet in a discreet place, anonymously." The scene then cuts to a dark silhouette of a man in profile, backlit by warm interior lights, before shifting to a bustling Parisian street with traffic and a newspaper kiosk displaying a 'LIU JO' advertisement. A male voice, speaking with a slight accent, explains, "I tried to crack the model to understand how this model works and what I could learn for my own business," continuing in French, "pour ma propre entreprise," before elaborating on financial models and advertising budgets, noting, "there are no expenses in advertising displays or television." The video transitions to a television screen showing a man in a white sweatshirt receiving a newspaper and later smiling as he walks outside a building. The female voice returns, analyzing, "Such positioning is unique in the fashion world," and discussing the highly competitive textile market, citing H&M and Zara as examples, where H&M "calls in singers or football players to create a buzz." Upbeat, jazzy background music briefly plays. The female voice continues, explaining that other retailers spend significantly on advertising, but "Not Zara." A different female voice then speaks rapidly in French, "Zara c'est pas une marque qui est portée sur les red carpets," which the first female voice translates, "Zara is not a brand that is worn on red carpets. So they went for a low profile." The French voice adds, "C'est rare moi. They built themselves on other..." as the segment concludes with a woman with gray hair, wearing a black top and white scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, airy room with white columns.

The segment opens with a woman in a black top and white scarf, speaking directly to the camera in a bright, airy room, explaining that brands advertise because they are not inherently desired, unlike a 'coveted brand' like Zara. As she speaks, the video transitions to close-ups of fashion products, including a cream-colored knitted sweater with ribbed detailing, vibrant orange high-heeled sandals with a buckle strap, and a black strappy high-heeled sandal with cut-out details, while an upbeat, light background music starts. The commentary shifts to Zara, describing it as 'a different brand' and highlighting its visionary founder, Ortega, who 'revolutionized fashion' by inventing 'fast fashion,' producing and delivering in 'record time' without traditional seasonal collections. Visually, an exterior shot of a modern ZARA storefront on a bustling city street shows urban pedestrians, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood and a man in a white shirt, walking past. The speaker notes Zara's ability to stock a store just '15 days after it has been drawn,' and that items are 'permanently out of stock' as part of their system. A woman is then seen exiting the ZARA store with a black shopping bag, followed by another woman in a light blue top adjusting her collar while holding a black-and-white striped bag. The scene then shifts to a dimly lit café interior, where a dark silhouette of a man is seen in profile, backlit by warm interior lights, creating a mysterious atmosphere. The camera slowly pans to reveal more silhouettes of people seated at tables, with a chandelier hanging above and arched windows showing the street outside. A male voiceover concludes the segment, explaining that unlike normal textile stores where being out of stock is problematic, 'not at Zara,' because 'out of stock is part of their marketing investment.'

The segment opens with a mysterious silhouette of a man in profile, backlit by warm interior lights in a dimly lit café, creating a contemplative atmosphere. This visual is interspersed with a brightly lit clothing store displaying neutral-toned garments, where a woman browses, and later returns to the café, revealing a chandelier and arched windows, emphasizing the contrast between dark interiors and illuminated backgrounds. Over these scenes, a male voice discusses the frustration that drives customers back to stores, noting the atypical speed at which products become available. A female narrator then highlights the brand's rapid marketing of new models, a point further elaborated by another male voice explaining that while the fashion industry typically takes 40 weeks from design to market, Zara achieves this in just two to four weeks, making their process "extremely quickly." The scene then shifts to a bustling Parisian street with pedestrians crossing in front of a ZARA storefront, a visual backdrop to the ongoing discussion about Zara's efficiency and customer loyalty, with the female narrator noting that Zara customers visit stores 17 times a year compared to competitors' 3-4 times. Finally, the camera focuses on individual women: a woman in a gray beanie and green coat states in French (translated) that she visits Zara "three to four times a week," followed by a woman in a camouflage jacket and sunglasses confirming in English that she shops at Zara "about three times a week," and a woman with curly blonde hair gesturing as she mentions "sometimes in the United States..." All these spoken testimonials reinforce the brand's high customer engagement.

event_blocks.summary: The video begins on a vibrant city street outside a ZARA HOME store, where a woman in a black polka-dot top and a woman in a striped shirt converse and laugh, mentioning "Each week" and "Oh yeah, yeah, no." The scene transitions to urban pedestrians passing a ZARA storefront, as a female narrator explains Zara's invention of "ultra-fast supply, fast fashion." A wide shot of the Arc de Triomphe and an 'ESSEC' sign follows, with the narrator noting Zara's strategy is taught in business schools "like here in Paris." Inside an ESSEC Business School lecture hall, Jérôme Barthelemy, a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, presents to students, with the projector screen displaying French questions about Zara's strategy, including 'Amancio Ortega est-il un visionnaire?' and 'Comment Zara parvient-elle à être rentable alors qu'elle produit ses vêtements en petites quantités et en Europe?'. Jérôme gestures while speaking in French, with an English voice-over explaining that most companies are "doomed to mediocrity," while Zara's originality lies in adapting to trends and producing similar-looking products in under 15 days; natural light streams in from windows, revealing an atrium with people walking above.

ZARA storefront.description: A modern ZARA storefront with large glass windows and a sign reading 'ZARA' in metallic letters, located on a city street with mannequins dressed in dark, stylish outfits visible inside.

projector screen with French text.description: A white projector screen displaying French questions in black text, including 'Amancio Ortega est-il un visionnaire?', 'Qu'est-ce que la « fast fashion »?', and 'Comment Zara parvient-elle à être rentable alors qu'elle produit ses vêtements en petites quantités et en Europe?', indicating a business case study on Zara.

ESSEC Business School.description: A modern building with a large 'ESSEC BUSINESS SCHOOL' sign, shown in a wide shot with the Arc de Triomphe in the background, indicating an academic or business setting.

Arc de Triomphe.description: A large, white stone arch monument in Paris, visible in the distance as traffic flows through the Champs-Élysées, with trees lining the avenue and vehicles moving under traffic lights.

woman in striped shirt.description: A woman with dark hair pulled back, wearing a black-and-white striped button-up shirt, standing beside the woman in the polka-dot top and speaking to the camera, heard saying 'Oh yeah, yeah, no' and laughing.

urban pedestrians.description: Pedestrians walking past the ZARA storefront on a city street, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood and a man in a white shirt, contributing to the bustling urban atmosphere.

lecture hall.description: An indoor classroom with students seated at tables, facing a projector screen displaying French questions about Zara’s business strategy, with natural light coming through large windows overlooking an atrium.

woman in black polka-dot top.description: A woman with blonde hair, wearing a black sleeveless top with white polka dots and a gold necklace, standing next to another woman in front of a ZARA HOME store, smiling and speaking to the camera, heard saying 'Each week' and laughing.

man in dark blazer and light blue shirt.description: Jérôme Barthelemy, a man with short dark hair, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, identified as 'a professor and specialist in corporate strategy,' giving a presentation in a lecture hall, gesturing expressively as he speaks in French with an English voice-over about Zara's strategy, including how they spot trends and produce items in less than 15 days.

raw_segments: The video opens on a lively city street in front of a ZARA HOME store, where two women, one in a black polka-dot top and the other in a black-and-white striped shirt, smile and speak to the camera, with their conversation including phrases like "Each week" and "Oh yeah, yeah, no" punctuated by laughter. The scene quickly transitions to a bustling urban environment, showing pedestrians, including a woman in a black coat with a fur-lined hood and a man in a white shirt, walking past a ZARA storefront. A female narrator's voice explains that Zara invented "ultra-fast supply, fast fashion," a model now adopted by many other brands. The camera then shifts to a wide shot of the Arc de Triomphe in Paris, with modern buildings and a large 'ESSEC' sign visible, as the narrator highlights that Zara's unique winning strategy is taught in most business schools, "like here in Paris." The scene moves indoors to a lecture hall at ESSEC Business School, where Jérôme Barthelemy, a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, identified by the narrator as "a professor and specialist in corporate strategy," is giving a presentation to a group of students. The projector screen displays French questions about Zara’s business strategy, such as 'Amancio Ortega est-il un visionnaire?' and 'Comment Zara parvient-elle à être rentable alors qu'elle produit ses vêtements en petites quantités et en Europe?'. Jérôme gestures expressively as he speaks in French, with an English voice-over explaining how most companies are "doomed to mediocrity" and Zara's originality lies in taking "received ideas and common practices" to follow trends, adapt, and get products looking alike in less than 15 days. Natural light streams in from large windows behind him, revealing an atrium with people walking above, emphasizing the global reach and academic analysis of Zara’s business model.

event_blocks.summary: Inside a dimly lit ZARA store, customers browse clothing racks as a male voice discusses fashion trends. The scene shifts to an exterior night shot of a modern office building, overlaid with '1963,' followed by a female narrator questioning Zara's rapid production and trend adherence, suggesting a visit to the 'Inditex headquarters,' described as a 'fortress.' An 'INDITEX' sign glows at night before a silhouette of a man appears, followed by a close-up of a man with long dark hair in a black blazer, speaking animatedly in front of an abstract painting. He narrates an anecdote about Amancio Ortega refusing to meet Prince Philippe of Spain, sending Jose Maria Castellano instead, and discusses Ortega's social connections and their link to the company's growth. The scene then shows an aerial view of a coastal cityscape, possibly A Coruña, Spain, before the female narrator returns. The narrative continues with a close-up of a cluttered metal shelving unit with a 'FAST FASHION' poster, as the narrator explains the difficulty of accessing Zara facilities. A woman with short, dark, curly hair types on a MacBook in a home office, while the narrator notes unsuccessful attempts to secure interviews and filming authorizations. An email from INDITEX declining a visit due to 'la complexité de l'emploi du temps de la société' is displayed. A woman in a red-and-white plaid shirt attempts to reach the communication service by phone, receiving a friendly but ultimately unhelpful response, leading the narrator to state, 'Zara is mute.' The man with long dark hair is heard saying, 'Zara is one of the few companies that has a very good communication team for not communicating.' The exploration of the Inditex website reveals news of Zara's expansion and profit increase. In a bustling fashion design studio, described as 'the heart of a war machine,' Inditex produces 'around 1 billion clothes annually.' The man with long dark hair calls the production process 'marvelous,' and an English male voiceover translates, describing the efficiency of employees cycling due to '110 kilometers of paths and rails' within a highly automated warehouse where boxes for 'ZARA NEW YORK N.Y.' are sorted by robotic systems. These tracks send '5 million clothes to Zara stores every week worldwide,' with the goal 'always to gain more time.' A man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt is seen speaking in a bright room, and the automated warehouse is shown again. The man with long dark hair reinforces that 'Creation and production, everything's done internally in order to go as fast as possible.' The segment opens with a close-up of a mannequin's lower body, then shows the man with long dark hair speaking in Spanish, followed by an English female voice stating, 'For that, Zara employs an army of designers.' Hundreds of designers globally observe fashion trends with cameras, or more accurately, 'a simple notebook and a pencil,' traveling to places like Tokyo, New York, Paris, Milan, and Singapore. A modern fashion studio is shown, followed by a close-up of a fashion sketch. In a cluttered workshop, a woman operates a vintage Singer sewing machine and another cuts fabric with blue-handled scissors, as the narrator explains that in A Coruña, 250 designers work on collections, totaling over 18,000 different models sold annually. An older woman with curly hair, wearing a checkered blazer, describes Ortega as 'a very simple person, intuitive with brilliant ideas,' who 'wanted to democratize fashion' and 'came from a very poor family,' while holding a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA.' The video begins with a panoramic view of A Coruña, identified as the home of Amancio Ortega, who has 'remained faithful all his life.' The scene transitions to a waterfront promenade and a highway, with a digital sign displaying 'OLEIROS,' a suburb of A Coruña. A building with a 'Refugio' sign is identified as a neighborhood restaurant where Ortega has simple habits. Inside the bar, a man in a suit and tie, who has known Ortega for 40 years, describes him as 'a normal person, super discreet,' 'a character,' and someone who 'deserves the money he has.' The segment concludes with the older woman, seated in a blue ergonomic chair, holding the 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' book, speaking directly to the camera.

coastal cityscape.description: A wide shot of a modern coastal city under a cloudy sky, featuring a large green field, high-rise buildings, a water tower, and a curved road leading to a circular building, explicitly identified by the calm, informative female narrator as A Coruña, where Amancio Ortega has lived all his life.

INDITEX sign.description: A large, illuminated sign with the word 'INDITEX' in white letters on a dark, geometrically designed structure, shown at night, representing the corporate identity whose website is explored by a female voiceover.

man with long dark hair.description: A man with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a black blazer over a light blue shirt, speaking expressively in both Spanish and English about designers and global fashion trends in front of a large abstract painting with black and red splatters on a wooden wall, gesturing with his hands.

modern office building.description: A multi-story, curved building with extensive glass facades and warm interior lighting, set against a green lawn, overlaid with the red text '1963', serving as a visual transition point with background music.

abstract painting with splatters.description: A large white canvas with black and red paint splatters and a signature in the corner, mounted on a wooden wall behind the man with long dark hair, serving as a backdrop for the interview setting.

ZARA store interior.description: A dimly lit retail space with clothing racks, warm overhead lighting, and customers browsing, creating a modern and inviting shopping environment.

silhouette of a man.description: A dark silhouette of a man standing against a deep blue background, creating a mysterious and dramatic visual.

metal shelving unit.description: A silver metal shelving unit filled with stacks of papers, files, and a framed 'FAST FASHION' poster, indicating an office or research setting.

email screenshot.description: A screen capture of a French email from INDITEX, dated October 27, 2015, declining a visit due to scheduling complexity, visually confirming the narrator's statement about 'dozens of calls and emails' and 'unsuccessful' requests for interviews.

woman with short, dark, curly hair.description: A woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing glasses and a black turtleneck, typing on a MacBook in a bright home office with bookshelves and a checkered rug.

plaid shirt.description: A person wearing a red-and-white plaid shirt, seen writing in a notebook and later talking on a landline phone at a cluttered desk.

woman in red-and-white plaid shirt.description: A woman with dark hair and glasses, wearing a red-and-white plaid shirt, engaged in a phone conversation at a desk, with a photo of Amancio Ortega and ZARA sales figures in the foreground. Her voice is heard, speaking with a slight accent, attempting to reach Zara's communication department and interacting with a receptionist.

Valoriza Facilidades sign.description: A blue sign with white text reading 'Valoriza Facilidades' mounted on a building facade, visible behind the man with long dark hair as he speaks.

automated warehouse.description: A high-tech warehouse with conveyor belts, robotic arms, and tall shelving units, where boxes labeled for Zara stores in cities like New York and Paris are sorted and transported efficiently, described by voiceovers as having 110 kilometers of tracks used to send 5 million clothes weekly worldwide, highlighting its speed and internal operations.

man in dark blazer and light blue shirt.description: A man with short dark hair, wearing a dark blazer over a light blue shirt, seen speaking expressively in a bright, airy room with large windows, likely during a lecture or interview, though his voice is not heard directly, only background music and voiceovers.

fashion showroom.description: A bright, minimalist 3D-rendered fashion showroom with white mannequins in black outfits, standing in a clean, white space with pink abstract light patterns on the walls, suggesting a digital or conceptual design environment.

sketchbook with fashion illustrations.description: A hand drawing a detailed fashion sketch of a long, structured garment with a marker on a white sheet of paper, showing precise lines and design elements.

workshop setting.description: A dimly lit, cluttered room with wooden floors, a radiator, a table covered in sewing materials, and people engaged in garment-making activities.

blue-handled scissors.description: A pair of scissors with bright blue handles, used by a woman to cut light-colored fabric in the workshop.

patterned fabric.description: Light-colored fabric with a subtle grid or checkered pattern, being cut and sewn in the workshop.

older woman in blue chair.description: A woman with curly hair, wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf with red and white floral designs, seated in a blue ergonomic chair in front of a bookshelf filled with books, speaking and gesturing while holding and flipping through a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA'. She is the primary, calm, and informative female narrator, providing extensive context about Ortega, A Coruña, and the locations visited, with her voice heard throughout much of the segment, including a distinct Spanish phrase at the end.

vintage Singer sewing machine.description: A black, classic-style sewing machine with a shiny metal handwheel and the brand name 'Singer' visible, used by a woman in a dimly lit workshop.

Refugio sign.description: A large, stylized sign with the word 'Refugio' in cursive script, mounted on the facade of a building with a modern design, identified by the narrator as a "neighborhood restaurant" in Oleiros where Amancio Ortega has his habits.

bar interior.description: An indoor space with a well-lit bar, wooden furniture, and patrons seated on high stools, featuring a man in a suit and tie speaking, with background chatter and clinking sounds audible.

man in suit and tie.description: A man with white hair, wearing a dark suit and a patterned tie, standing near the entrance of the bar, speaking and gesturing. He is identified by the narrator as "the boss" who has known Ortega for 40 years. His voice is heard in Spanish (muffled) and then translated/interpreted in English, describing Ortega as "a normal person, super discreet," "a character," and someone who "deserves the money he has."

highway with road signs.description: A moving shot from a vehicle along a city bridge, passing under a tall white tower, then panning to a waterfront promenade with palm trees and a distant cityscape, indicating a coastal urban environment.

OLEIROS sign.description: A digital sign displaying 'OLEIROS' in yellow letters, mounted on a pole beside a road, identified by the narrator as the location of the 'Refugio' restaurant, a suburb of A Coruña.

raw_segments: The segment opens inside a dimly lit ZARA store, where customers browse clothing racks under warm, focused lighting, creating a modern retail atmosphere, as a male voice in French briefly discusses what works in fashion within 15 days. The scene then transitions to an exterior night shot of a sleek, multi-story modern office building with illuminated glass facades, overlaid with the red text '1963'. A female narrator in English questions how Zara produces so quickly and stays on trend, suggesting a visit to the 'Inditex headquarters,' which she describes as a 'fortress' where few outsiders, including journalists, have met the 'enigmatic Ortega.' The camera pans across the contemporary architecture before cutting to a glowing 'INDITEX' sign at night. A silhouette of a man appears against a deep blue background, followed by a close-up of a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, who speaks animatedly in English in front of a large abstract painting with black and red splatters. He gestures expressively while holding up a fashion magazine, narrating an anecdote where Amancio Ortega refused to meet Prince Philippe of Spain, sending Jose Maria Castellano instead. This man further elaborates on Ortega's social life, mentioning his connections to the daughter of Infanta Elena and King Juan Carlos of Spain, and his interactions with government heads, linking these to the company's growth. The scene then shifts to a wide aerial view of a coastal cityscape under a cloudy sky, featuring a curved road, a water tower, and a modern circular building, suggesting A Coruña, Spain. The female narrator returns to discuss 'the future King of...' before a blurred transition ends the segment, accompanied by light, investigative background music throughout.

The segment opens with a close-up of a cluttered metal shelving unit, featuring stacks of papers and files, and a framed 'FAST FASHION' poster, as a female narrator explains that 'Spain is not welcomed in A Coruña as he should be.' The scene transitions to a woman with short, dark, curly hair, wearing glasses and a black turtleneck, typing intently on a silver MacBook in a bright home office, while the narrator continues, 'In our case, we have no chance of getting into Zara.' A close-up follows of a hand in a plaid shirt writing in a notebook, then another close-up of hands typing on a laptop keyboard, as the narrator adds, 'Yet, it's not for lack of trying. Despite dozens of calls and emails.' The focus shifts to a screen displaying a forwarded email in French from INDITEX, dated October 27, 2015, declining a visit due to 'la complexité de l'emploi du temps de la société,' with the narrator confirming, 'Our requests for interviews with officials, and our authorizations to shoot in shops or factories of the Spanish group were unsuccessful.' Upbeat, slightly tense background music begins. The scene cuts to a woman in a red-and-white plaid shirt, wearing glasses, talking on a landline phone at a cluttered desk, with a framed photo of Amancio Ortega and ZARA sales figures prominently displayed. The camera slowly zooms in on her focused expression as a phone rings, then clicks. The woman, speaking with a slight accent, asks, 'I would like to talk to someone of the communication, please,' to which a different female voice replies, 'Okay, I'll be through in one second, please.' The woman thanks her, and the phone rings again. The narrator comments, 'The communication service is reachable. The reception desk friendly, but then no answer. Zara is mute,' as the phone continues to ring. The final shot is of a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking animatedly in front of a large abstract painting, his voice heard stating, 'Zara is one of the few companies that has a very good communication team for not communicating,' as the background music continues.

The minute opens with a man speaking Spanish, stating "Very good communication team for not communicating," before an English female voiceover begins, explaining the exploration of the Inditex website. Upbeat, slightly mysterious background music starts as news headlines from the Inditex website highlight Zara’s expansion into New Zealand and South Korea, followed by a 46% profit increase. The scene transitions to a modern office building with '1963' overlaid, followed by a bustling fashion design studio where designers and staff are actively working with fabric, sketching, and discussing designs. The female voiceover describes this as "the heart of a war machine," stating Inditex produces "around 1 billion clothes annually." The camera cuts to a man with long dark hair, seated before an abstract painting, speaking expressively in Spanish, saying "It's marvelous." An English male voiceover translates, describing the production process at Inditex as "amazing" because "a lot of employees go around by bicycle" due to "110 kilometers of paths and rails." This is intercut with footage of a highly automated warehouse, where boxes labeled for destinations like 'ZARA NEW YORK N.Y.' move along conveyor belts and are sorted by robotic systems, emphasizing the efficiency of the supply chain. The female voiceover returns, explaining these tracks send "5 million clothes to Zara stores every week worldwide," with the goal "always to gain more time." The scene shifts to a man in a dark blazer and light blue shirt, speaking in a bright, airy room with large windows, though his voice is not heard directly. The video then returns to the automated warehouse, showing close-ups of labeled packages and automated sorting. The segment concludes with the man with long dark hair continuing his interview, as the English male voiceover reinforces that "Creation and production, everything's done internally in order to go as fast as possible."

The video opens with a close-up of a mannequin's lower body, showcasing a geometric-patterned skirt and a black handbag. The scene quickly transitions to a man with long dark hair, wearing a black blazer, speaking expressively in front of a large abstract painting. He is heard speaking in Spanish, followed by an English female voice stating, "For that, Zara employs an army of designers." A Spanish male voice then adds, "Hay muchísimos diseñadores," which is echoed by an English male voice confirming, "There are hundreds of designers around the world." The man continues to gesture as the English male voice describes designers traveling with "a camera," quickly corrected by a Spanish male voice to "con una sencilla libreta y un lápiz" (with a simple notebook and a pencil) to observe fashion. The English male voice then details how they "watch how the kids dress up in Tokyo" and "what they wear in New York," while a Spanish male voice adds "what's happening in Paris" and "what's happening in Milan," and both voices discuss "how they breathe in Singapore." The setting then shifts to a wide shot of a modern fashion studio or showroom, where several people are engaged in design work, followed by a close-up of a hand drawing a detailed fashion sketch. The scene changes again to a dimly lit, cluttered workshop where three people are working on garments, including a woman operating a vintage black Singer sewing machine and another cutting fabric with blue-handled scissors. During this, an English female voice explains, "In A Coruña, 250 designers work on the collections. In total, more than 18,000 different models are sold each year." The final segment features an older woman with curly hair, wearing a checkered blazer and a patterned scarf, seated in a blue ergonomic chair in front of a bookshelf. The English female voice continues, "Hard to believe that for Ortega, it all started in the dining room of his brother." The woman then speaks, alternating between English and Spanish, describing Ortega as "a very simple person, intuitive with brilliant ideas," who "had really clear ideas" and "wanted to democratize fashion," emphasizing that "he came from a very poor family" as she gestures and occasionally flips through a book titled 'AMANCIO ORTEGA' that she holds in her lap.

The minute opens with a sweeping panoramic view of a coastal city under a cloudy sky, showcasing residential buildings, green hills, and a harbor, all set to a calm, informative female narration. event_blocks.summary: An older woman, dressed in a checkered blazer and patterned scarf, speaks expressively in Spanish from a blue ergonomic chair before a bookshelf, stating, "He doesn't want to be seen. A part of him is so simple and real with humility... He does not want to show off... I haven't done all this alone. A lot of people have done it with me. And it's not fair that people think that I did it alone... that they think that I am the one who did it." As light, slightly mysterious background music begins, the scene shifts to a wide coastal cityscape under a cloudy sky, featuring modern buildings along a waterfront. A narrator explains that this "strategy of discretion and simplicity is respected by Ortega's bodyguards," and that "Inditex executives live in this soulless neighborhood in Laros," known as "Zarapolis." The camera focuses on a modern beige building, marked with an 'URBANIZACION ICARIA' sign and secured by private security, where a red car is parked and the number '13' is visible on a black door, exemplifying "uniform and airtight houses, all under surveillance." The view then moves to a rocky shoreline with pedestrians, followed by 'EL RELUJO RESTAURANTE,' identifiable by its red awning and outdoor patrons, which the narrator describes as the "quite simple restaurant" frequented by "Zaristocrates" – multi-millionaires who are "as low profile as their boss." Following a brief, blurry coastal vista, the video cuts to a busy city street with tall buildings, including a ZARA storefront, where urban pedestrians, such as a woman in a black coat and a man in a white shirt, pass by. The narrator concludes, "From here, you would not believe it, but those Zaristocrates are masters of the world," as the music intensifies dramatically, alluding to "Their empire" while showcasing a modern city street featuring a red 'Fiat' sign and a dome-topped structure.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins with a bustling urban scene outside a ZARA storefront under bright daylight, featuring urban pedestrians and a yellow taxi, as a female narrator introduces ZARA's global reach and comfortable, interchangeable clothing produced worldwide. The scene shifts to a man with long dark hair speaking expressively before an abstract painting, while the narrator explains ZARA's shift to globalized production due to growth, supported by a world map highlighting manufacturing regions in Asia, Spain, Portugal, Morocco, and Latin America. A man with shoulder-length dark hair, identified as Juan, head of a workers' association, enters a cozy café and discusses documents, a laptop, and a newspaper with fashion illustrations with two women. Juan contests ZARA's outsourcing figures, linking high Spanish unemployment to textile outsourcing, and states in Spanish, "This is an area where there were a lot of jobs here..." The scene transitions to a dimly lit café where Juan, now in a denim jacket, discusses Galicia's employment crisis and over 20% unemployment, with a laptop displaying news about labor and a fashion event. The narrator explains that textile giants like Inditex increase profits by producing where labor is cheaper, causing crisis in their home countries. Juan clarifies their fight is against slavery and multinational companies seeking the cheapest production, using Zara as an example. Juan, now in a brown jacket with a red-and-black backpack, walks through a dynamic urban environment, past a 'tackia' store and a 'BANCA' sign, crossing a pedestrian crossing, and smiles at the camera as the narrator states he is leading them to a Zara store to support his claims, suggesting they enter to check labels. Inside the ZARA store, the man browses clothing racks, and a male narrator challenges the myth of Spanish production, stating, "I bet you won't find here any labels that say made in Spain." The man examines garments, revealing 'MADE IN CHINA' and 'MADE IN PAKISTAN' tags, while the narrator lists manufacturing countries like Turkey and Morocco, emphasizing Pakistan as a place "where it's well known that workers have no rights." As the man exits the store, the narrator suggests leaving before they are seen, and outside, he passes the ZARA storefront and other businesses, smiling at the camera with his backpack as the female narrator concludes, "In the third world, textile giants do not always control their supply."

She continues, noting that 40 years after opening its first store, the world belongs to Zara, as upbeat, slightly mysterious background music begins. The segment opens inside a ZARA store, where a man with shoulder-length dark hair, wearing a brown jacket over a blue shirt, actively browses clothing racks. Amidst background music and indistinct chatter, a male narrator immediately challenges the myth that most ZARA production is Spanish, stating, "I bet you won't find here any labels that say made in Spain." The man meticulously examines garments, picking up a dark floral-patterned shirt and a cream-colored knitted sweater, revealing a 'ZARA MAN' tag and 'MADE IN CHINA' details. The narrator continues to list manufacturing countries like "Turkey," "Morocco," and "China," emphasizing the absence of "Spain" labels. event_blocks.summary: Scandals surrounding Zara's supply chain intensified in recent years, beginning with the 2005 Spectrum factory collapse in Bangladesh, a Zara subcontractor where 64 people died due to unsafe working conditions, prompting Inditex to establish a compensation fund. By 2011, Zara faced accusations of child labor and 'slave-like conditions' in Brazil, as highlighted by a MailOnline news article, leading to public protests and scrutiny, visually reinforced by a ZARA storefront and a close-up of a ZARA label on black clothing. The Alameda Foundation further documented 'sordid sweatshops' in Argentina, with Inditex acknowledging supplier misconduct. In response, Inditex strengthened its 'Code for manufacturers and suppliers' in 2012, detailing ethical standards like 'No forced labour' and 'Reasonable working hours.' The narrative then shifts to Tunisia, chosen for its lower wages compared to China, with a focus on the garment production hub near Monastir. Wassim Zalia, a former Levi's employee, invested 1500 euros to establish the 'SIP PLUS Confection' factory, which manufactures clothes for Zara. His facility, employing 350 people, predominantly women aged 16-35, operates 48 hours a week in compliance with Tunisian law. The factory is a hive of activity, with workers on Brother sewing machines producing a large order of 10,000 shirts for Zara, requiring rapid delivery. Wassim, accompanied by a supervisor in a white lab coat, meticulously checks the quality of the finished white shirts, confirming their authenticity as Zara products. The production process is highly organized, utilizing yellow and green plastic crates, and employs a 'working to the minute' system where each worker is timed, with a target of 38 minutes per shirt, to ensure efficiency and profitability, as exemplified by Jamila, who monitors production calculations and adherence to time targets.

raw_segments: The minute opens with a somber, slightly tense instrumental background music as a narrator details how "scandals multiplied" in recent years. Visually, a distressing scene unfolds with a green Caterpillar bulldozer amidst a rubble pile, surrounded by rescue workers, illustrating the 2005 collapse of the Spectrum factory, a Zara subcontractor in Bangladesh, which killed 64 people. The narrator explains these workers toiled seven days a week for just 10 euros per month, leading Inditex to set up a compensation fund. Four years later, the focus shifts to 2011, as the video shows a news article from MailOnline headlined 'Zara accused of employing children as young as 14 in 'slave labour' factories in Brazil'. The narrator confirms Zara is "still getting talked about," this time in South America, where suppliers employed workers in "slave-like conditions." This is followed by a ZARA storefront at night, where protesters hold signs, visually representing the public scrutiny. A close-up of a ZARA label on black clothing reinforces the brand's involvement. The narrator adds that in Argentina, the Alameda Foundation filmed raids in "sordid sweatshops," visually suggested by a dimly lit, cluttered room with a bed, TV, and a pile of black garments. Inditex acknowledged these irregularities, stating suppliers used these methods without informing them. The segment concludes with a computer screen displaying the INDITEX website's 'Code for manufacturers and suppliers' page, outlining ethical standards, as the narrator explains Inditex strengthened this code "to clean up its image."

The minute opens with a close-up of a computer screen displaying the INDITEX 'Code for manufacturers and suppliers' page, where a female narrator reads aloud ethical standards such as 'No forced labour', 'No child labour', 'No harsh or inhumane treatment', 'Reasonable working hours', 'The right to remuneration', and 'Environmental pledge', noting these were established in 2012. As rhythmic, investigative background music begins, the scene transitions to a digital map of North Africa, zooming into Tunisia, specifically pinpointing Tunis and Monastir. The narrator explains they went to Tunisia because wages are lower than in China and Inditex has many subcontractors there, with the main textile production center near Monastir, 200 kilometers from Tunis, where 450 garment enterprises are gathered. Visuals then show a coastal town with white buildings, a prickly pear cactus with red fruit, and three men carrying large white rolls of material along a roadside, passing a road sign pointing to 'Mahdia' and 'Monastir'. The narrator continues, stating they will see how clothing is produced for Zara, introducing Wasim Zalia, a former Levi's employee who invested 1500 euros to set up a textile factory and goes to Mahdia daily. The scene shifts to the interior of a moving car, showing Wasim Zalia in a green jacket driving, looking upward as the car travels along a tree-lined road. The final shot shows a black Mercedes-Benz with license plate '183-101-06' driving on a paved road, approaching a crosswalk, as Wasim Zalia speaks with an accent, stating, "On today, thank you God, I have 350 employees and I work with the Inditex group."

The segment opens with an upbeat, slightly exotic instrumental tune as a black Mercedes-Benz sedan, license plate '183-101-06', drives along a sunlit road towards the 'SIP PLUS Confection' garment manufacturing facility, identified by its Arabic and French sign as a customs-supervised factory. A female narrator reveals this is "Wassim's company, Sip Confection," which "mainly manufactures clothes for Zara." The man in a green jacket, Wassim, exits the car and enters the building, where he is greeted by a receptionist in a red hijab. Inside, the constant hum of sewing machines and general factory sounds fill the air. Wassim exchanges greetings in Arabic, saying "Sabah. Labas." and later "Ah, nedi, sba khir," as he proceeds into the large, brightly lit factory floor. The narrator explains that "here, as in the entire textile industry in Tunisia, 85% of employees are women," who are "between 16 and 35 years old, and work 48 hours a week" in accordance with Tunisian law, which Wassim respects. The factory is bustling with these women, in light blue uniforms and colorful hijabs, operating Brother sewing machines and assembling garments. The narrator specifies that "all the employees are working on a very big order of shirts for Zara," and Wassim and his foremen "must quickly deliver 10,000 pieces." Wassim walks through the production area, observing the workflow, and interacts with a supervisor in a white lab coat bearing the 'SIP PLUS Confection' logo. He asks for a shirt in Arabic ("A'tini shi qamis"), to which the supervisor responds "malfa." The supervisor then shows him racks of finished white shirts, individually wrapped in plastic. Wassim examines a garment closely, touching the fabric and checking the stitching, confirming in English, "This is a Zara shirt. It's a woman." The clean, well-organized environment, with fluorescent lighting and yellow storage crates, underscores the scale and direct oversight of the manufacturing process.

The segment opens with a close-up of a white garment, plastic-wrapped and tagged, hinting at a production environment. The scene quickly transitions to a bustling garment factory, filled with the constant whirring and clatter of industrial sewing machines, creating a methodical background hum. Multiple women, wearing colorful hijabs and light blue uniforms, are diligently seated at Brother sewing machines, guiding fabric with precision and speed. The supervisor guides him, showing stacks of finished white garments. A male voice explains that one worker is responsible for 'launching, labeling, each piece,' and that 'ironing' is also done here. The female narrator emphasizes the importance of this order for Wassim and the factory's future. The brightly lit factory features stacked yellow and green plastic crates, indicating an organized space. One worker, wearing a black hijab, is seen using a smartphone displaying a chronometer, which the female narrator explains is part of a system where 'each worker is timed,' called 'working to the minute.' The narrator then introduces 'Jamila,' a worker responsible for production calculations, who 'does not take her eyes off her watch,' as exceeding the 38-minute target would cause the company to 'lose money,' making workers subject to regular checks. event_blocks.summary: A factory worker holds a smartphone displaying a chronometer as a narrator explains performance tracking for cost-per-minute calculations and profitability, noting a €45 year-end bonus for high performers. The scene shifts to a bustling garment factory floor with women operating white Brother sewing machines, producing items that allow Wasim to profit and Zara to sell shirts at a significant markup, as evidenced by a €29.99 price tag on a shirt costing €3 to produce. The narrative moves to an exterior of a factory and a 'Denim Services Tunisie' sign, highlighting Tunisia's reputation for dyeing. Wasim outsources Zara shirt dyeing to Masdoor, 20 kilometers from Monastir. Inside Denim Services, Maya Dayouri, the director, oversees 100 employees. A supervisor in a white coat with a 'SIP PLUS Confection' logo guides a woman in a green jacket, showing her finished garments amidst ambient Arabic calls and machine sounds. The focus then shifts to the dyeing and fading process where a visitor in a green jacket observes an industrial washing machine being prepared for ecru dyeing of Zara shirts. The scene transitions to denim production where workers use spray guns for finishing. The supervisor details the use of 'toxic products' like 'chemical agents, fixators, acetic acid' for color, warning of their corrosive and dangerous nature, with visible safety signage for 'ACIDE ACETIQUE' and 'AGENT DE CATIONISATION,' noting these chemicals can cause diseases and cancers despite being handled with bare hands. The segment concludes with a close-up of a denim garment undergoing chemical treatment.

event_blocks.summary: In a cluttered workshop, a young man in a blue and black jacket adjusts his clothing before a woman in a white lab coat, hair tied up, joins him and gestures while speaking, as a pair of jeans hangs from a white industrial machine labeled 'OXY' with Arabic text 'مصنع'. A female narrator expresses concern over management's apparent lack of concern for worker protection. The scene transitions to a close-up of a large circular machine, then to a worker in a white coat inspecting and folding denim garments. Subsequently, a worker in a teal shirt uses a spray gun to apply a chemical finish to jeans, highlighting the denim's texture and stitching. During this process, the woman in the lab coat questions the worker, "Why do you not wear gloves?" to which the worker replies it's "not practical" as he wants to "work fast," despite the woman's warnings that it's "dangerous for your skin" and the product is "very dangerous for your hand." The narrator elaborates that performance is paramount, with workers risking their jobs if they don't work quickly, compelling them to prioritize speed over their health. The worker in the teal shirt is then observed pouring liquid from a white container into a large blue barrel bearing a hazard symbol, within an industrial setting featuring pipes, metal structures, and chemical storage, indicative of a garment finishing or dyeing facility. The narrator concludes by raising questions about the toxicity of the chemicals employed in the dyeing process.

event_blocks.summary: A female voiceover introduces the environmental impact of textile factories, predominantly situated along the Bay of Monastir, as the scene opens with a coastal cityscape featuring white and beige buildings, dry reeds, and a clear blue sky, underscored by somber, investigative music. Mounir Hassine, President of the Tunisian Forum for Economic and Social Rights, identified as a man in a dark brown coat and glasses, exits a dusty silver car on a sunlit, arid roadside adjacent to a busy highway. Walking along a dirt path beside a weathered concrete curb, Hassine gestures towards the environment, his voice rising above the sounds of passing traffic. He points to a concrete drainage ditch filled with murky water and plastic bag debris, exclaiming about the water used for washing jeans and stating that waste is "poured directly here" from "three" factories located "there," which dump into this "canal." Amidst passing trucks, a yellow taxi, and a white van on the highway, Hassine gestures towards the traffic and the polluted ditch, emphasizing the "contributions made by those factories that are dumping here directly." He stresses that this untreated waste "goes directly into the bay, haphazardly, just like that, you see?" The sequence concludes with Hassine near the highway, gesturing towards "the coast there" and "all those plants," highlighting the ecological disaster.

event_blocks.summary: A man in a dark brown coat and glasses, speaking with a French accent beside a busy highway, gestures toward a polluted drainage ditch, lamenting that the bay was once safe and an asset. The camera reveals an arid wetland with dry reeds and stagnant, murky water, showing a submerged yellow plastic bottle and black plastic bag in the ditch, which a narrator identifies as Monastir's Bay, now the "Triangle of Death" due to untreated waste and chemical discharge. The man, now near a white truck and buses with highway traffic, states that authorities are aware of the pollution but do nothing, linking it to employment concerns, and questions what can be done if pressuring factories leads to unemployment. The narrator explains that global demand for cheap clothes has a cost and environmental protection is not the government's priority. The scene shifts to a sunlit factory courtyard where women in light blue uniforms and hijabs, some with children, are seated, and a supervisor in a white coat with a 'SIP PLUS Confection' logo walks through, as the narrator notes that "Amon Tiga employs 150,000 people around the globe," connecting the industrial setting to the employment versus environmental protection dilemma, all underscored by somber background music.

event_blocks.summary: The narrative begins in a sunlit factory courtyard, where women in casual attire and colorful hijabs socialize, setting a backdrop for commentary on supplier ethics. A black Mercedes-Benz sedan enters a dimly lit underground parking garage, and Ortega, a man in a dark suit, emerges and walks away, with the narrator revealing it's his 80th birthday and a surprise awaits. The environment dramatically transforms into a modern, well-lit lobby with a reflective, bubble-like ceiling installation and polished floors, accompanied by upbeat pop music. Ortega and a woman in a long black coat emerge from a glass elevator to enthusiastic applause from a diverse group of people. An announcer welcomes Ortega, and the man and woman share a warm, emotional embrace, the man placing his hand on her back and the woman resting her head on his shoulder. man in dark suit.description: A man with white hair, wearing a dark suit, who emerges from the elevator, embraces the woman, and walks hand-in-hand with her through the lobby and hallway. A female narrator identifies him as 'Monsieur Teget' and reveals his backstory: at 13, he promised his mom she would never be humiliated again due to poverty, and by 80, he became the richest man in the world, displaying emotional warmth and leadership amidst the celebration.

glass doors.description: Transparent glass doors with metallic frames, through which the couple walks from the lobby into a bright hallway, symbolizing a transition to a new phase or space.

woman in long black coat.description: A woman with blonde hair tied back, wearing a long black coat over a white top and jeans, who embraces the man and walks with him through the lobby and hallway, sharing a moment of emotional connection.

group of people.description: A diverse group of men and women in business and casual attire, standing and clapping enthusiastically in the modern lobby and hallway, observing the emotional reunion between the man and woman with expressions of joy and admiration, their continuous applause contributing significantly to the celebratory audio.

modern lobby.description: A well-lit, modern interior with a polished floor, reflective bubble-like ceiling installation, and white pillars with uplighting, where the couple reunites and is applauded by a group of people, all set to the backdrop of upbeat pop/dance music and enthusiastic clapping.

raw_segments: The video opens with a vibrant and emotionally charged scene in a modern, well-lit lobby, immediately filled with the energetic sounds of upbeat pop/dance music and enthusiastic clapping. A male vocalist sings encouraging phrases like "Come on!" and "Are you ready?" as a man in a dark suit with white hair, later identified by a female narrator as 'Monsieur Teget'—who, at 13, promised his mom she'd never be humiliated again due to poverty and by 80 became the richest man in the world—emerges from a glass-enclosed elevator. He embraces a woman in a long black coat over a white top and jeans, and they walk hand-in-hand through the lobby, which features a reflective bubble-like ceiling installation and white pillars with uplighting. The diverse group of people gathered continues their enthusiastic clapping and cheering, their admiration palpable as the music swells with lyrics like "It's up to you, what's your pleasure, everyone." The couple then walks through transparent glass doors into a bright white hallway, continuing their journey hand-in-hand, with the celebratory music and clapping persisting, symbolizing a joyful transition to a new phase. The audio gradually fades out, leaving silence before the video concludes with a black screen displaying the 'girafprod' logo, a white circular emblem with a black giraffe silhouette and the text 'girafprod' in lowercase, followed by the copyright notice: '© 2022 - GIRAF PROD - ALL RIGHTS RESERVED'.

Simultaneously, the audio track is silent, lacking any speech, music, or ambient sounds. This sustained void in both sensory inputs signifies a static pause or transition within the narrative.
